paragraphs.text: Hello, I am a C++ programmer and I would like to share my view on a large number of topics related to digital fairness: 01 - "App stores": As a developer, I find these app stores to be like mandatory landlords. Services like the play store and steam are so ubiquitously used that even if you have the ability to distribute your software without their help, the habit many consumers have to look up the name of something on said platforms means you lose a lot of sales if you do not, even if you don't use the additional features these platforms provide. "Steam", for example, is the "nicest" of these stores, but even if all of your customers arrive from external means such as social media, and even if you do not use any of their features - such as networking, achievements, or social integration - they will still take a 30% cut. I would like to see the cut be much lower (~5% or less) for -ALL- of these "app stores" if the only service they provide is downloads and reviews. I do not think it is fair for most of these stores to take a 30% cut off every sale. Even pushing the argument further, payment processors and credit cards should also take a much lower cut as all they are doing is changing numbers in a database, an operation which costs much less than a fraction of a cent for these companies, yet take a 1-3% cut for every transaction, often with a minimum of 1. 02 - Death of "owned" videogames/software: I am not going to go into depth on this one, since many other people already have - the "Stop killing games" public movement has more information than I could ever hope to provide, and is easy to search. 03 - The "capture" of users: In a very large amount of cases, companies make an attractive product that attracts a lot of new users, only to later make the product worse with ads or higher prices, using their acquired leverage to cash in. There are many examples of this practice: Apple had great success with their phones, yet attempting to transfer files from any Apple device to a computer requires more than just a cable, making migration very painful; Another example is Windows, who is increasingly toying with the idea of displaying ads (which they have done in the home screen for the game Doom: the dark ages) and advertising to users by having OneDrive integrated into the file browser by default - despite it not actually being a file or folder, not to mention making microsoft accounts start to be built into the operating system - which is not at all necessary. In all of these cases, regulating these companies by allowing for a "export my data" option would go a long way to remove the leverage these companies have on users. Deciding what the export format should be for each type of device (operating system [windows, apple devices...], social media [discord, facebook...], app store [steam, origin, epic games store...], content platforms [youtube, twitch...] etc) would not be a simple task especially without restricting future innovations, but it would go a long way in allowing small startups to compete with very large userbases. 04 - FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out) in videogames: A very large amount of games nowadays use limited-time events to attract players and make older players return to the game. Some examples (but far from being the only ones) are: Path of Exile ("leagues"), Overwatch (events and "seasons"), Rocket League ("seasons"), Fortnite, League of Legends... This practice has proved to be such a successful business model that it is becoming even more ubiquitous. Within this practice, there are a lot of strategies. Some games use "battle passes" which give exclusive rewards if you play a certain amount. Other games give out a reward if you reach a certain leaderboard rank within the "season". The worst offenders in this category are often a game genre called "Gacha games" (e.g. Genshin Impact), which offer a limited-time digital slot machine which offers rewards that sometimes do not reappear for 3-5 years.
